turns.content: I've been reading the official docs on how to manage dependencies in Rust but I still don't get why the minimum dependencies philosophy is so important. It just feels like there's a lot of stuff that isn't being used.

That's a great question because it goes straight to the heart of Rust project hygiene. The core reason is performance and maintainability; every extra dependency adds compile time, runtime overhead, and potential security holes. When you use tools like cargo-tree or cargo-udeps, you're pruning the tree so that only what your code actually calls remains in the final binary. This reduces surface area for bugs and keeps dependencies minimal.

But I think I understand now that it's about removing unused crates from the dependency graph, but isn't there a risk of breaking things if we just delete them? What if something in one crate depends on another indirectly?

That is exactly why you must verify before deleting; blindly removing crates can break the build. The safe way to do this is by analyzing your dependency tree with cargo-tree first, then using cargo-udeps to identify truly orphaned nodes that aren't referenced anywhere in your codebase or transitive dependencies. This ensures you only remove what's genuinely unused without accidentally breaking the build.
Okay so I run cargo-tree, see some deep branches, then use cargo-udeps to find them and delete them? But how do I know which ones are safe to remove without guessing?

You don't guess; you let the tools tell you. When you run cargo-tree, it shows the full graph of crates including indirect dependencies. Then cargo-udeps analyzes that and lists only those crates with no incoming references from your code or any other crate in the tree. Those are the safe ones to delete because they contribute nothing to your build. The remaining crates stay even if their version is outdated.
So it's not just about removing old versions but actually cutting off branches that don't matter at all? That makes sense for keeping the project lean and secure.

Exactly. It is specifically about pruning dependency trees to remove anything that isn't reachable from your main code, which keeps your Cargo.toml clean and prevents accidental exposure of unused functionality or vulnerabilities. This minimalism makes debugging easier too because you have fewer crates to track down when something breaks.

I've read about the minimum dependencies philosophy but I still don't fully understand why it matters. My CI keeps failing because of some obscure dependency conflict, and I'm wondering if shrinking my tree would help.

That's a great instinct, and honestly, reducing your dependency graph is one of the best ways to stabilize builds. The core idea is keeping only what you actually need so that transitive dependencies don't get in the way. You can visualize this using cargo-tree to see exactly how things are connected, and if you find packages you aren't touching with cargo-udeps, removing them often fixes those phantom conflicts.

I thought adding the Json wrapper to our project was enough, so I just ran cargo add serde_json and assumed that would cover all my serialization needs without pulling in other crates. But the dependency tree looks bloated with unnecessary items.

You're right to be concerned about that growth; while serde_json is essential for parsing JSON, it often pulls in a lot of optional dependencies like regex or std::str::FromStr implementations when you aren't using them. The solution here isn't just adding more crates but actively auditing what your code actually imports.

I don't get why removing those unused items would make the build faster if they were already there, so I'm not sure how deleting a crate from Cargo.toml helps performance at all. Just tell me that cargo remove is enough to fix it and move on.

Removing crates from Cargo.toml isn't just about shrinking the file; it's about breaking transitive dependencies that you never call, which reduces memory pressure and compilation time by avoiding resolving features you don't need. The real gain comes from using tools like cargo-udeps to find these dead edges before they bloat your binary size.

Okay so I'll run cargo-udeps but what exactly does it output that tells me which crates are actually harmful versus just harmless noise? It seems like it might just list everything without distinguishing between critical and non-critical dependencies.

cargo-udeps generates a detailed report showing the actual usage paths for each dependency, so you can see exactly where an unused crate is being referenced or if it's truly dead code. This distinction matters because removing a genuinely unused crate shrinks your final binary by cutting out the symbols and data structures those crates would otherwise contribute.
